title: Verbs with Fixed Prepositions (Feste Präpositionen)
description: Many German verbs are permanently paired with a specific preposition and case. Unlike two-way prepositions, the case here does not change based on meaning — it is fixed and must be memorized with each verb. These verb-preposition combinations are essential for fluent German and cannot be guessed from English translations.
whenToUse: When expressing actions, emotions, or states that require a specific preposition in German
When the English equivalent uses a different preposition or no preposition at all
When forming questions or referring back to things using wo-/da- compounds
When writing or speaking at B1 level and above, where fixed prepositions are expected

rules.title: Verbs with Fixed Prepositions + Accusative
rules.explanation: Many common verbs always take a specific preposition followed by the accusative case. The preposition and case must be learned together with the verb as a unit. The accusative case here is fixed — it does not depend on movement or location.

examples.explanation: 'warten auf + Akk' means 'to wait for'. German uses 'auf', not 'für' as you might expect from English.

examples.correct: Sie freut sich auf die Ferien.
examples.explanation: 'sich freuen auf + Akk' means 'to look forward to'. Note: 'sich freuen über + Akk' means 'to be happy about' (something that already happened).
'denken an + Akk' means 'to think of/about someone'. 'Denken über' exists but means 'to have an opinion about'.

rules.title: Verbs with Fixed Prepositions + Dative
rules.explanation: Other verbs always take a specific preposition followed by the dative case. Again, this is fixed and must be memorized — the dative is not triggered by location but by the verb-preposition combination itself.

examples.correct: Ich treffe mich mit meinem Freund.
examples.incorrect: Ich treffe mich mit meinen Freund.
examples.explanation: 'sich treffen mit + Dat' means 'to meet with'. 'Mit' always takes dative: meinem Freund (not meinen).

examples.correct: Sie hat Angst vor der Prüfung.
examples.explanation: 'Angst haben vor + Dat' means 'to be afraid of'. 'Vor' here always takes dative in this fixed expression.

examples.correct: Er träumt von einem neuen Auto.
examples.incorrect: Er träumt über ein neues Auto.
examples.explanation: 'träumen von + Dat' means 'to dream of/about'. German uses 'von', not 'über'.

rules.title: Da- Compounds (darauf, dafür, darüber, ...)
rules.explanation: When referring back to a thing (not a person), German replaces the preposition + pronoun with a da-compound. If the preposition starts with a vowel, 'dar-' is used instead of 'da-'. Da-compounds are never used for people — use the preposition + personal pronoun instead.

examples.correct: Ich warte auf den Bus. → Ich warte darauf.
examples.explanation: For things, use the da-compound 'darauf' (da + auf). 'Auf es' is not standard German.

examples.correct: Sie interessiert sich für Musik. → Sie interessiert sich dafür.
examples.explanation: 'dafür' replaces 'für Musik' when referring back to it. Da + für = dafür.

examples.correct: Er denkt an seinen Bruder. → Er denkt an ihn.
examples.explanation: For people, do NOT use a da-compound. Use the preposition + personal pronoun: 'an ihn' (not 'daran').

rules.title: Wo- Compounds (worauf, wofür, worüber, ...)
rules.explanation: To ask a question about a thing with a fixed preposition, use a wo-compound. If the preposition starts with a vowel, 'wor-' is used. For people, use the preposition + 'wen/wem' instead.

examples.correct: Worauf wartest du? — Auf den Bus.
examples.explanation: 'Worauf' (wo + auf) is the standard way to ask 'what are you waiting for?'. 'Auf was' is colloquial but not standard.

examples.correct: Wofür interessierst du dich? — Für Kunst.
examples.explanation: 'Wofür' (wo + für) asks 'what are you interested in?'

examples.correct: An wen denkst du? — An meinen Bruder.
examples.explanation: For people, use preposition + 'wen' (Akk) or 'wem' (Dat), NOT a wo-compound. 'Woran denkst du?' would ask about a thing.

rules.title: Distinguishing Similar Verb-Preposition Combinations
rules.explanation: Some verbs change meaning depending on which preposition they are paired with. Paying close attention to the preposition is critical for understanding and producing the correct meaning.

examples.correct: sich freuen auf + Akk = to look forward to / sich freuen über + Akk = to be happy about
examples.explanation: 'Ich freue mich auf den Urlaub' (looking forward to the vacation, future). 'Ich freue mich über das Geschenk' (happy about the gift, present/past).

examples.correct: sprechen mit + Dat = to talk with / sprechen über + Akk = to talk about
examples.explanation: 'Ich spreche mit meinem Chef' (talking with my boss). 'Ich spreche über das Problem' (talking about the problem).

examples.correct: denken an + Akk = to think of / denken über + Akk = to think about (have an opinion)
examples.explanation: 'Ich denke an dich' (I'm thinking of you). 'Was denkst du über den Film?' (What do you think about the film?).

tables.title: Common Verbs with Fixed Prepositions
rows: Wir freuen uns auf das Wochenende.
Er bereitet sich auf die Prüfung vor.
Sie erinnert sich an den Urlaub.
Ich gewöhne mich an das Wetter.
Sie entscheidet sich für das rote Kleid.
Sie kümmert sich um die Kinder.
Ich ärgere mich über den Lärm.
Ich treffe mich mit meiner Freundin.
Wir fangen mit der Arbeit an.
Sie erkundigt sich nach dem Preis.
Das Kind hat Angst vor Hunden.
to belong to / be part of

tables.title: Da- and Wo- Compounds
rows: Ich warte darauf. → Worauf wartest du?
Ich denke daran. → Woran denkst du?
Ich interessiere mich dafür. → Wofür interessierst du dich?
Es geht darum. → Worum geht es?
Ich ärgere mich darüber. → Worüber ärgerst du dich?
Ich fange damit an. → Womit fängst du an?
Ich träume davon. → Wovon träumst du?
Ich suche danach. → Wonach suchst du?
Ich habe Angst davor. → Wovor hast du Angst?
Das gehört dazu. → Wozu gehört das?

commonMistakes.explanation: German uses 'warten auf + Akk', not 'warten für'. This is a common transfer error from English 'wait for'.
'Denken an + Akk' means 'to think of someone'. 'Denken über' means 'to have an opinion about' and is used differently.
In standard German, use wo-compounds for things: 'worauf', not 'auf was'. The split form is colloquial.
When referring to things, use da-compounds (darauf), not preposition + 'es'. 'Auf es' is not standard.

commonMistakes.incorrect: Er denkt daran. (when meaning a person)
commonMistakes.explanation: Da-compounds refer to things only. For people, always use the preposition + personal pronoun: 'an sie', 'an ihn', etc.

commonMistakes.correct: Ich freue mich auf den Urlaub. (future event)
commonMistakes.incorrect: Ich freue mich über den Urlaub. (when meaning 'looking forward to')
commonMistakes.explanation: 'sich freuen auf' = looking forward to (future). 'sich freuen über' = being happy about (present/past). Mixing them up changes the meaning.

tips: Always learn verbs together with their preposition and case as a unit: 'warten auf + Akk', not just 'warten'. Write them in your vocabulary notebook this way.
Use da-compounds (darauf, dafür, darüber) for things and preposition + pronoun (auf ihn, für sie) for people. This is a strict rule in standard German.
Create flashcards with the verb + preposition + case on one side and an example sentence on the other. Regular review is the most effective way to internalize these combinations.
When you encounter a new verb-preposition combination in a text, note whether it takes accusative or dative immediately. Checking later is less effective than noticing in context.
Pay special attention to verbs that change meaning with different prepositions: 'sich freuen auf' vs. 'sich freuen über', 'sprechen mit' vs. 'sprechen über', 'denken an' vs. 'denken über'.
